data.title: Henkel To Buy Premium Hair Care Brand OLAPLEX For $1.4 Bln
data.description:
DUESSELDORF (dpa-AFX) - German chemical and consumer goods major Henkel AG & Co. KGaA (HENOY, HENKY, HEN.DE) announced Thursday that it has entered into a definitive agreement to acquire OLAPLEX, a premium hair care brand, for $2.06 per share. The total transaction value would be $1.4 billion.

The deal has been unanimously approved by OLAPLEX's Board of Directors, and its controlling shareholder Advent has entered into a written consent to approve the transaction.

The acquisition is subject to customary closing conditions, including regulatory approvals.

The acquisition is part of Henkel's purposeful growth agenda and aims to further expand hair care as a core category within its Consumer Brands business.

OLAPLEX premium channel hair care brand holds a portfolio of science-led, high-performance products. OLAPLEX's sales is split between the U.S. and key international markets. In fiscal year 2025, OLAPLEX generated around 370 million euros in sales and delivered a strong gross margin.

Henkel CEO Carsten Knobel stated, 'The planned acquisition of OLAPLEX is fully in line with Henkel's strategy to expand its portfolio through compelling, value-adding M&A activities. This transaction allows us to expand our presence in premium hair care. The brand creates compelling opportunities for future growth and innovation.'

On the XETRA in Germany, Henkel shares were trading at 63.40 euros, up 0.24 percent.

data.title: Delivery Hero's Free Cash Flow Impresses — Market Talk
data.description: Delivery Hero's 2025 free cash flow of 250 million euros, which beat consensus views of 201 million euros, was respectable, RBC Capital Markets analyst Wassachon Udomsilpa writes. The German food-delivery company expects like-for-like gross merchandise value growth over 2026 to be in the range of 8% to 10%, which compares with consensus views of 9% growth, she writes. Meanwhile, it expects adjusted Ebitda of between 910 million and 960 million euros versus consensus views of 951 million euros. Segment revenue growth is seen between 14% to 16%, versus consensus of 10% growth, she adds. Shares trade flat at 15.84 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Rohm, Toshiba, Mitsubishi Elec to begin power chip integration talks, Nikkei says
data.description:
Japan's Rohm , Toshiba and Mitsubishi Electric will start talks to integrate their power semiconductor businesses that can form the world's second-biggest power chip group after Infineon , the Nikkei newspaper said on Thursday.

The three Japanese chipmakers may announce the commencement of the integration talks as early as on Friday, which could affect rival Denso's bid to acquire Rohm, Nikkei reported.

data.title: South African Rand Falls Ahead of Rate Decision — Market Talk
data.description: The South African rand falls as the Middle East conflict weighs ahead of an upcoming interest-rate decision from South Africa's central bank. Iran's public denial about U.S. claims of constructive talks between the two sides raises doubts over a resolution to the war any time soon. Higher oil prices and lower gold prices resulting from the conflict are negative for South Africa's terms of trade. The rate decision is due at 1300 GMT. The central bank is expected to leave rates on hold but the focus is on the outlook for future decisions. "The Iran conflict does provide good reasons to remain cautious today," Commerzbank's Volkmar Baur says in a note. The dollar rises 0.5% to 17.0279 rand. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Prices rebound after Iran says will not hold talks with U.S. to end the war
data.description:
Key points:
  • Iran says it has no intention of holding talks
  • Concerns raised again about longer duration of energy flow disruption
  • Colder weather in north-west Europe forecast

Dutch and British wholesale gas prices rose on Thursday morning, rebounding from the previous session, as Iran said it would not hold talks to end the war, raising concerns again about a protracted duration of energy flow disruption.

The benchmark Dutch front-month contract at the TTF hub (TFMBMc1) rose by around 1.90 euros to 54.73 euros per megawatt hour (MWh) by 0849 GMT, ICE data showed.

The British front-month contract (NGLNMc1) was 5.24 pence higher at 138.01 pence per therm.

U.S. President ​Donald Trump said Iran was desperate to make a deal to end nearly four weeks of fighting, contradicting the Iranian foreign minister who said his country was reviewing a U.S. proposal but had ​no intention of holding talks to wind down the conflict.

The conflict has all but halted shipments of oil and liquefied natural gas (LNG) from the Gulf region and QatarEnergy has cut LNG capacity by 17% at its Ras Laffan facility which was damaged in attacks.

"Optimism regarding a ceasefire seems to be fading as many market participants doubt that Iran can accept the conditions imposed by the United States, which at the same time threatens to hit it harder if it fails to accept the proposal," said analysts at Engie EnergyScan.

Meanwhile, supply disruptions linked to the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran could delay construction of liquefied natural gas projects slated for development in the U.S., Freeport LNG CEO Michael Smith said on Wednesday at the CERAWeek energy conference in Houston.

In the short term, temperatures in north-west Europe are forecast to stay low until the weekend and then gradually rise to normal levels, LSEG data showed.

In the European carbon market, the benchmark contract (CFI2Zc1) inched down by 0.03 euro to 70.77 euros a metric ton.

data.title: Norway Jobless Rate Rises To 6-month High
data.description:
OSLO (dpa-AFX) - Norway's unemployment rate increased further in February to the highest level in six months, the labor force survey data from Statistics Norway showed on Thursday.

The seasonally adjusted jobless rate rose to 4.9 percent in February from 4.6 percent in January. Moreover, a similar rate was last seen in August 2025.

In the corresponding month last year, the rate was 4.1 percent.

There were 150,000 unemployed people in February, up from 141,000 in the previous month.

Data showed that the employment rate also increased to 70.0 percent from 69.1 percent.

data.title: Trump's EU Trade Deal Faces Key Test in Parliamentary Vote — WSJ
data.description:
By Kim Mackrael

A trade agreement with the European Union that President Trump called the "biggest of all the deals" will face a key test Thursday when European lawmakers vote on whether to support tariff cuts for a range of American imports.

The European Commission, the bloc's executive body, agreed last year to cut tariffs on U.S. industrial goods and some agricultural products, part of a deal that also included a U.S. pledge to cap tariffs on most EU goods at 15%. The EU tariff cuts can't go ahead without approval from the European Parliament, where some lawmakers have expressed opposition to the plan.

Trump's ambassador to the EU, Andrew Puzder, warned in an interview with WSJ this week that the U.S. could increase its tariffs on the bloc if lawmakers don't back the proposed cuts. "If they don't approve this, our tariffs aren't going to stay at the Turnberry agreement levels," Puzder said, referring to the village in Scotland where the U.S. and EU announced their trade deal last summer.

Thursday's vote isn't the end of the line for the tariff reductions. If lawmakers back the legislation, the EU's parliament, EU capitals and the Commission will hold negotiations to determine its final text. If lawmakers vote against the legislation, it will go back to a parliamentary committee.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: London's Miners Fall as Investors Await Progress on Middle East Peace Deal — Market Talk
data.description: London's miners slide in opening trade as gold prices slip and investors wait for clear signs that progress is being made on a peace agreement between the U.S. and Iran. Gold is down 2.4% to $4,442 a troy ounce and is coming under pressure as investors dial back expectations of the U.S. Federal Reserve cutting interest rates. Lower borrowing costs typically support non-yielding assets like gold. Higher inflation and interest rates will also hit sentiment and demand for metals. Precious metals miner Hochschild Mining falls 6% while peer Fresnillo drops just over 4%. Copper miner Antofagasta and diversified miner Anglo American are both down around 4.2%. Commodities giant Glencore drops 2.5%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Czech cenbank ready to hike rates if risk of rising core inflation, minutes show
data.description:
Czech central bankers would be ready to hike rates if there were a risk of rising core inflation and a shifting inflation path due to the war in the Middle East, minutes from the Czech National Bank's last policy meeting showed on Thursday.

The CNB left its main interest rate (CZCBIR=ECI) at 3.50% at its meeting a week ago but said it was keeping its options open. In February, before the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran began, the bank's board had discussed possible monetary easing.

The bank agreed at its March 19 meeting that the conflict's macroeconomic impact was difficult to assess right now. The magnitude of the shock, its duration and the speed of normalising commodity prices after the acute phase of the conflict ends are all key issues, the meeting minutes said.

"(Governor) Ales Michl said it was necessary to keep monetary policy tight and not underestimate the cost shock," the minutes said.

"Hawkish policy was in order. If there were a risk of rising core inflation, the Board would be ready to tighten monetary policy."

Inflation fell below the bank's 2% target in January and February, helped by a government measure to ease people's energy bills.

The board said the shock of the conflict - which has driven up global energy prices - was coming at a time of low inflation and relatively high interest rates, which provided a buffer. It would be premature to consider raising rates, the minutes said.

Vice Governor Eva Zamrazilova said evidence that inflationary pressures were passing through to other price categories, especially in a longer-lasting conflict, would be necessary.

Board member Jan Prochazka "emphasised that the CNB would not hesitate to react" if the inflation path shifted in the medium term, the minutes said.

Zamrazilova and Vice Governor Jan Frait said that without the Iran conflict, some anti-inflationary factors - such as the weaker performance of some euro zone economies, a slightly stronger crown and signs of a slowdown in services inflation - would have warranted a discussion about lowering rates.

data.title: Next's Update Looks Reassuring Despite Challenging Backdrop — Market Talk
data.description: Next PLC gave a cheering update amid a challenging environment due to the war in the Persian Gulf, Jefferies analysts say. The U.K. retailer seems to have had a strong start to the year, excluding the disruption in the Middle East, the analysts write in a note. The group said the performance in its home market during the first eight weeks of fiscal 2027 was encouraging. However, this was offset by the disruption in the Middle East, the analysts say. This region accounts for 6% of group sales. The company could pass costs through as higher pricing if the conflict persists. "We see this update as reassuring on the strong U.K. trading and implied ability to pass-through costs," the analysts say. Shares rise 6.4%. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)
